By 2025-10-26, the strongest official evidence is that BIS rescinded/non-enforced the January AI Diffusion Rule in May and promised a future replacement, while issuing guidance and policy statements rather than a clearly qualifying broad new EAR rule. With only about two months left in 2025, a YES still has a real path—BIS has ongoing policy momentum on advanced chips and could issue a late interim final rule covering chips, cloud/data centers, or model weights—but the absence of a visible post-May broad Federal Register rule or near-final pipeline makes that less likely than not. I land slightly below the market at 27%.

I estimate a 5% chance. By 2025-10-26, almost all substantive-sounding AI bills were still only introduced or referred to committee, leaving very little time for passage through both chambers and signature. The main plausible path is the FY2026 NDAA: the Senate passed a version containing the GAIN AI Act, which imposes binding rules on AI-tailored chip exports. But that provision was absent from the House bill, faced White House pushback, and may not even clearly satisfy the resolution criteria because it is more semiconductor/export-control policy than direct regulation of AI systems. With no other advanced qualifying bill visible, YES looks possible but very unlikely.

Very unlikely. The strongest direct evidence is that coalition leaders repeatedly framed any deployment as a post-hostilities or post-war reassurance force, while late-October reporting still showed Russia rejecting ceasefire terms and Europe/Ukraine only preparing new proposals. Planning appears real and includes possible air/sea roles, but there was no clear evidence by 2025-10-26 of actual activation inside Ukraine, its airspace, or territorial waters. With only weeks left in 2025, the remaining path is a sudden ceasefire plus rapid launch of a limited qualifying mission, which seems possible but remote.

Past precedent keeps this live: European states have already donated M270-family systems to Ukraine, and continued pressure to offset uncertain U.S. support could still produce a symbolic late-2025 announcement. But the strongest late-2025 evidence points the other way: public reporting emphasizes European-funded missiles for existing HIMARS, EU-wide financing, and domestic recapitalization/procurement (Germany buying PULS, Estonia/Poland expanding Chunmoo, UK expanding M270) rather than new launcher transfers. With only about two months left after the cutoff, constrained European stocks and the lack of any clear 2025 launcher pledge in reporting make YES less likely than NO.

By late Oct 2025, the path to YES is almost entirely a Q4 story. Reported/derived 2025 data put Q1 around 10–10.5 bcm, H1 around 19.3 bcm (so Q2 about 9 bcm), and Jan-Sep around 28.8 bcm when combining ~13.8 bcm TurkStream with ~15 bcm LNG, implying Q3 around 9.5 bcm. That means no quarter through Q3 was close, and Q4 would need to jump above 15 bcm—higher than the cited Q4 2024 peak of 14.125 bcm—even though the Ukraine transit route ended on Jan. 1, 2025, leaving only TurkStream plus LNG. The main reason not to go lower is that Russian LNG still flowed, the EU delayed restrictions, and winter could lift Q4 purchases. But given the observed 2025 run rate and the European Commission expectation of only ~36.5 bcm total Russian gas imports in 2025, a 15+ bcm quarter looks very unlikely.

I estimate an 84% chance. The strongest dated evidence before the cutoff points to Musk having re-established a large lead by early-to-mid October 2025: Reuters reported he crossed $500B in net worth on Oct. 2 (search_3_result_0), and a mid-October report still had him around $453B versus Larry Ellison around $367B (search_1_result_3). The main counterevidence is that Ellison briefly overtook Musk on Bloomberg in September (search_0_result_4, search_0_result_8), which shows the ranking is not locked in. But that overtake appears to have lasted only hours before Musk regained #1 (search_0_result_8, search_4_result_6). Over roughly two months, an incumbent with an estimated ~80B+ lead is usually favored to stay on top. I keep it below 90% because the resolution source is Bloomberg rather than Forbes, and Musk’s wealth is tied to volatile assets like Tesla and private-tech valuations, so a large December swing is still plausible.

Iran had both motive and capability to threaten Hormuz in 2025, and the market’s broad resolution criteria mean even partial interdiction of Western shipping could count. But the strongest evidence is negative: during the much hotter June Israel/Iran/U.S. crisis, commercial traffic still flowed, maritime authorities reported the strait remained open, and by October the threats looked more like deterrent signaling tied to oil-export sanctions than imminent operational preparation. With only about two months left after the cutoff, YES seems to require a fresh major trigger plus unusually aggressive Iranian escalation. I put that tail risk at 9%.

I estimate a 5% chance. The strongest pro-YES factor is that India and Pakistan had a serious military crisis in May 2025 and the ceasefire was described as fragile, so the dyad clearly retained escalation risk. But the market requires something much rarer than clashes or even limited war: a formal declaration of war. The available evidence points the other way. After the worst fighting in decades, both sides accepted a ceasefire, later discussed extending it, and Reuters reported troop reductions near the border. Historically, India and Pakistan have repeatedly fought without formal declarations, and my searches found no strong late-October evidence of a renewed crisis or legal/political preparation for a declaration. With only about two months left in the year at the cutoff, YES looks possible but quite unlikely.

About a 6% chance. Over the final ~2 months of 2025, a founder-CEO of a mega-cap firm usually has a low departure rate absent a major shock. Musk-specific risk is higher than average because there was credible mid-2025 succession chatter and investor pressure, but the strongest and most recent evidence points the other way: Musk said he plans to remain CEO for five years, Tesla’s chair publicly backed him, and in Aug-Oct 2025 the board was still approving/advancing enormous retention-focused compensation plans and expressing confidence in his leadership. That makes a voluntary or board-driven exit by year-end unlikely. The remaining tail risk is mostly an exogenous shock or abrupt rupture after the November shareholder meeting.

Tesla had a real Austin robotaxi service by the cutoff, plus a public app/waitlist, which means the product is no longer just a paper launch. But the key problem is that, as of late October 2025, Tesla still appears to be operating with in-car safety monitors in Austin, and California/Bay Area rides also had a human monitor. Under this market’s rules, those rides likely do not count. That means Tesla would need to remove observers very soon after the cutoff and then rack up 10,000 bona fide rides in only the remaining weeks of 2025 while staying active on Dec. 31. Musk said monitors would be removed by year-end, but that is still a plan rather than accomplished fact, and there are meaningful headwinds: early driving mistakes, operational restrictions, modest cumulative mileage by late July, a small initial fleet, and active NHTSA scrutiny of both FSD and the Austin robotaxi deployment. The upside case is that Tesla already has the app, riders, and Austin operations in place, so if it actually flips to Waymo-style no-one-in-car service in early December, 10,000 rides is operationally plausible. Still, given the timing compression and disqualification risk from any remote-human-backup setup, NO is more likely than YES.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the evidence strongly favored YES. The key update is that South Korea’s official preliminary 2024 TFR was already 0.75, above the 2023 benchmark of 0.72. So for NO to happen, 2025 would need to reverse sharply back below 0.72. But multiple indicators pointed the other way: 2024 births and marriages both rose materially, Q1 2025 births were up about 7.3% y/y with Q1 TFR at 0.82, and by mid-2025 births were still rising year over year for 13 consecutive months. There was also an official policy-committee forecast around 0.79 for 2025. The main caution is that some forecasts came from interested government actors and part of the rebound may reflect temporary post-COVID marriage timing rather than a durable trend. Still, given the threshold is only 0.72, the observed 2024-2025 momentum makes a drop back below it unlikely.

As of 2025-10-26, Musk is still Tesla CEO in official company materials, the board has publicly denied actively replacing him, Reuters reported he said he intended to remain CEO for another five years, and Tesla’s board was still proposing/approving enormous CEO compensation packages in Aug–Oct 2025 aimed at retaining him long term. The main pro-YES evidence is the spring 2025 report that the board had explored succession options plus shareholder pressure, but that looks outweighed by later, more authoritative evidence of board support and retention efforts. With only about two months left in 2025, a YES resolution would likely require a sudden forcing shock (health, legal, or governance), which seems possible but unlikely. I therefore put the chance slightly above the market’s 3% at 5%.

As of 2025-10-26, the strongest evidence is that the major 2025 US-Iran military episode had already happened in June and then ended quickly in a ceasefire, rather than maturing into the kind of sustained, publicly acknowledged US-Iran war this market requires. Reuters and CRS consistently described a 12-day June war ending in a U.S.-backed ceasefire on June 24, and by October Trump was still publicly saying the war was over and that the U.S. was ready for a deal. There were still escalation pathways—especially snapback sanctions, Hormuz threats, and the fact that the U.S. had already directly struck Iran once in 2025—but with only about two months left in the year and no strong evidence by late October of renewed sustained direct hostilities, the chance of crossing the market’s relatively high YES threshold before Jan. 1 looked low but not zero.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, authoritative pre-cutoff reporting indicates San Diego FC had already clinched an MLS playoff berth, secured the No. 1 seed in the Western Conference, and were already in a Round One playoff matchup. AP reported they were 'playoff-bound' and had clinched in 28 games, while MLS reported they earned the No. 1 seed and had a Round One series set. Given the question is simply whether they make the playoffs in 2025, this is as close to resolved-YES as forecasting rules allow; the only residual doubt is possible market-specific wording ambiguity, not the sports fact itself.

A new human moon landing by end-2025 looks extraordinarily unlikely. The only serious U.S. path, Artemis III, had already slipped beyond 2025 in NASA’s March 2024 schedule and was then delayed again to mid-2027 by December 2024; Artemis II, the necessary precursor crewed lunar flyby, also slipped into 2026. NASA reporting in August 2025 still showed Artemis III hardware integration milestones extending into spring 2026. China’s official crewed lunar target remains before/by 2030, with key systems still in prototype development in 2025. Recent 2025 reporting also says SpaceX’s HLS is tied to Artemis III no earlier than 2027, with NASA safety advisers warning it could be years late. So there is no credible, publicly visible mission architecture that could put a human on the lunar surface before the deadline. I would put the true probability below 5%, but given the platform’s probability floor I submit 5%.

I estimate a 5% chance. By late October 2025, the best pre-cutoff evidence suggests TSLA was still around the low-$400s: a report on Oct. 21 implied a current price near $443, and Sep. coverage described a 52-week range from about $212 to $488. That means the stock would need to fall more than 50% in roughly two months to print below $200. Tesla is volatile and had already suffered a near-50% drawdown earlier in 2025, so the chance is not zero, but multiple sources place the year's low above $200 and even technical support far above that threshold. The post-earnings reaction in late October looked like a contained selloff rather than the start of a crash. Relative to the 4% market estimate on Oct. 16, I land only slightly higher because Tesla’s tail risk is real, but the hurdle is still very large.

Trump had already won by the cutoff, so the question is effectively about whether the relevant 2025 BLS inflation reading available by Dec. 10 would be below 2.5%. As of the cutoff, the latest official CPI was still 3.0% y/y for September, and most late-2025 forecasts pointed to inflation staying around 2.7-3.1%, not falling sharply below 2.5%. On top of that, the October 2025 government shutdown created a serious risk that the key next CPI release(s) would be delayed or degraded. That means the market needed both a sizable disinflation surprise and timely official publication. There is still a narrow path via energy/base effects, but it looks unlikely.

Trump already did not win the 2025 Nobel Peace Prize, so this market is effectively the inverse of the Ukraine ceasefire question: it resolves YES if there is no bilateral ceasefire before 2026. As of 2025-10-26, the strongest evidence points to no deal: Russia rejected an immediate ceasefire, reiterated prior maximalist terms, a Trump-Putin summit was put on hold/canceled, new U.S. sanctions were imposed, and fighting was still active. There is still some chance because Europe/Ukraine were preparing a current-lines ceasefire proposal and Trump said a ceasefire was still possible, but with only about two months left and positions still far apart, I put the chance of no ceasefire at about 86%.

I estimate about a 19% chance. Waymo is genuinely mainstream by late 2025 and has produced several absurd, parody-friendly storylines (school-bus probe, neighborhood conflicts, stalled cars, viral antics), and there are still multiple SNL episodes left in 2025. But brand-specific SNL sketches remain uncommon, early season 51 coverage was dominated by politics rather than autonomous-vehicle comedy, and if SNL does parody this topic it may use a generic robotaxi/self-driving-car premise without specifically featuring Waymo.

As of 2025-10-26, Musk had clearly announced the "America Party," but the stronger evidence pointed to non-formation: no verified filings at launch, fake/disavowed FEC filings, no official website or official party social account, Axios calling it "failing to launch," and Reuters later reporting he was quietly putting the brakes on the project to focus on his companies. Given the market’s loose criteria, a fast symbolic launch in the final two months of 2025 was still possible, but it looked unlikely. So YES was a low-probability tail outcome rather than the base case.

Iran faced an unusually bad 2025: war damage, sanctions snapback, economic stress, factional infighting, and renewed concern about Khamenei’s health all raise the chance of a sudden break above a normal base rate. But as of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the strongest evidence still points to regime resilience rather than imminent collapse. Reuters reported no significant anti-regime protests even after the June war, and the state responded with effective repression and checkpoints; by October, central institutions were still meeting in Tehran and passing legislation (search_3_summary). Expert assessments also suggested an immediate coup or collapse was unlikely, and succession mechanisms plus IRGC continuity would probably preserve the system even if Khamenei became incapacitated (search_4_summary). With only about two months left in 2025 and no clear evidence of mass defections or loss of control in Tehran, YES is possible but remains unlikely.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the evidence strongly suggests hallucinations are improving a lot but not 'fixed' in the broad practical sense this market seems to require. Near the cutoff, academic surveys still describe hallucinations as an ongoing vulnerability rather than a solved problem, including an October 2025 survey saying LLMs remain vulnerable (search_0_result_8). Independent grounded-evaluation results in 2025 still show meaningful hallucination rates on real summarization tasks—around 6.3% for Gemini 2.5 Flash and roughly 15-16% for GPT-4o and Claude 3.7 Sonnet (search_2_result_6). Vendor-side evidence is also mixed: GPT-5 is reported to hallucinate less (search_3_result_1), and some benchmark results for top models are around 1% on narrow tasks (search_1_result_9), but OpenAI also reported major regressions for some reasoning models such as o3 at 33% on PersonQA and said more research is needed to understand why (search_1_result_3). Since the resolution hinges on whether the hallucination problem is 'fixed' rather than merely reduced, the most likely judgment is that a significant outstanding problem still exists by end-2025.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Doe v. Bonnell was still actively and acrimoniously litigated: discovery and sealing fights were ongoing, plaintiff had recently been allowed to amend, the court had expressed skepticism about urgent relief, and there was no clear sign of mediation, a settlement conference, or a dismissal/stay for negotiations. Civil cases often settle eventually, so a late-year deal remained possible, but with only about two months left and threshold/jurisdiction issues still live, settlement by 2025-12-31 looked distinctly less likely than not.

Nigeria was nowhere near 95% on any credible pre-cutoff metric. The strongest narrow measure is CBN’s report that only 52% of adults had recognized bank accounts in 2023. Even broader measures were far below target: EFInA put formal financial inclusion at 64% and total inclusion at 74% in 2023, and a 2024 Global Findex-based figure put Nigeria’s account ownership at 63.3%. With Nigeria still identified in 2024 as one of the major homes of the world’s unbanked adults, there is effectively no plausible path to 95% bank-account ownership before 2025. I therefore pin this at the floor probability.

I put this at the floor because the only plausible path to YES by end-2025 is that existing CBD geoengineering decisions are judged to create treaty-based commitments for many parties. But the best legal evidence I found cuts against that: CBD decision X/33 uses soft wording ('invites'), later decisions 'urge' implementation, and general legal analysis says COP decisions are usually not binding unless the parent treaty clearly authorizes new obligations. Meanwhile, the more concrete alternative pathways look weak: the scholar-led Non-Use Agreement is not an intergovernmental treaty, UNEA failed to agree even on governance language in 2024, and the London Protocol marine-geoengineering amendments were still not in force by 2025. Political momentum among African and other Global South countries is real, but converting that into a binding agreement with 10+ countries in the final weeks of 2025 looked highly unlikely.

By late Oct. 2025, the evidence is far short of the resolution bar. There is a real peer-reviewed literature around IM1 spherules, but the main formal result is a 2024 Chemical Geology classification paper, not a peer-reviewed systematic review concluding with 95% confidence that the material is both non-Earth and technological. Multiple published critiques offer terrestrial/natural explanations and challenge the trajectory/localization link to IM1. The hoped-for path to stronger evidence—a second expedition recovering larger diagnostic fragments—still appeared contingent in 2025, and experts argued such high-speed material should have vaporized. So YES now seems possible only via an extremely fast, last-minute, dramatic publication that overturns the contested state of the evidence.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the available evidence points strongly away from a broad Mag-7 bubble pop. Multiple sources indicate a substantial recovery after the spring selloff: by July, Nvidia, Microsoft, and Meta were at or near record highs, with Amazon and Alphabet only slightly off records (search_1_result_2; search_1_summary). Apple then made a fresh record high on Oct. 20, 2025, and the group’s combined market cap hit an all-time high on Oct. 9, 2025 (search_3_result_1; search_2_result_1). The spring slump also appears not to have come close for four names: Tesla was the worst hit, while most others were down far less, and Apple’s maximum reported 2025 drawdown was only 31% (search_0_summary; search_3_summary). So a YES would require either an unobserved earlier episode where four names briefly crossed 50% below ATH despite all this recovery evidence, or a historic synchronized crash in the final two months of 2025. That’s a tail risk, but not zero.

The likeliest NO path is that the CDC’s commonly cited US estimate remains around 35,000-37,000 annual deaths, far below 70,000, and there are only about two months left in 2025. The main YES path would be a broader non-CDC modeled estimate (especially 'associated with AMR' rather than 'attributable to AMR'), since that methodology yields much larger counts globally and regionally. But after several targeted searches of CDC, Lancet, and IHME/GRAM sources, I did not find an explicit US-specific figure at or above 70,000, and the summaries repeatedly emphasize the definitional gap. So YES is possible via a broader credible-source estimate or a surprise late CDC revision, but still unlikely.

La evidencia decisiva es que Rockstar anunció oficialmente el 2 de mayo de 2025 que Grand Theft Auto VI pasa a lanzarse el 26 de mayo de 2026, en lugar de otoño de 2025. Dado que Europa normalmente formaría parte del lanzamiento estándar autorizado, esa demora oficial hace muy improbable que el juego esté a la venta en Europa en cualquier momento de 2025. Dejo una pequeña probabilidad residual solo por escenarios atípicos: una reversión extraordinaria del calendario, una venta anticipada muy inusual, o alguna interpretación limítrofe sobre disponibilidad/preventa que calificara para la resolución. Pero, con la información disponible hasta 2025-10-26, NO es claramente el resultado más probable.

Bitcoin was already far above the $93,429.20 threshold by early/mid-October 2025: Reuters reported a new ATH above $125k on Oct. 5 and, even after a sharp liquidation-driven selloff, BTC only fell to about $104.8k and was still around $113.1k on Oct. 14. That means NO would require roughly another 10%–20% decline from late-October levels by Dec. 31. That is very possible for Bitcoin, especially given macro/trade-war shocks and active demand for downside hedges, but it is still less likely than staying above the threshold given strong institutional ETF inflows and continued product launches. So I land a bit above 4-in-5 for YES, not higher because 2025 already showed BTC can trade below $90k during risk-off episodes.

This is a low-probability YES. The outside view is already tiny: Atlantic hurricanes stronger than 160 mph are rare in any month, and very late-season cases are rarer still. The authoritative evidence I found up to Oct. 26, 2025 shows the basin was still active, with Hurricane Melissa ongoing, but the NHC information I could verify had Melissa around 80 kt (~90 mph) on Oct. 25 and forecast only to about 105 kt (~121 mph) by Oct. 26, well short of the >160 mph threshold. Same-day NHC discussion also pointed mainly to SW Caribbean convection rather than a clearly organized, high-end new threat. Historical context pushes further down: Iota’s 160 mph peak in mid-November 2020 was noted as the most intense Atlantic storm that late in the season, and the 1932 Cuba hurricane is cited as the only November Category 5. So a >160 mph hurricane after Oct. 26 would require either Melissa or a new storm to dramatically outperform a very unfavorable late-season base rate. I land at 5%, essentially a floor-level but nonzero chance.

Chegg looks genuinely distressed, but a bankruptcy filing before Jan. 1, 2026 would require fairly imminent liquidity or maturity pressure. The evidence I found points to a shrinking, disrupted business that is restructuring and exploring strategic alternatives, yet still generating positive adjusted EBITDA in Q2 2025 and having already dealt with the most immediate debt pressure by repaying the 2025 converts and reducing the 2026 notes. That combination makes bankruptcy plausible in a longer horizon, but less likely in the remaining ~2 months after the cutoff.

As of 2025-10-26, the case for a YES resolution looks extremely weak. The original headline RK anomaly that motivated the question no longer appears to be the path to discovery; 2025 review literature says the earlier RK/RK* excitement has cooled and updated measurements are consistent with the SM. The main surviving LFU hint is the charged-current R(D)/R(D*) sector, but 2025 sources still describe the world tension as only about 3.1σ, far short of the 5σ threshold. A 2025 Belle II LFU test is also SM-consistent at just 1.1σ. Just as important, the resolution requires not merely a strong preprint or conference talk but a peer-reviewed paper published before 2025-12-31, leaving only about two months from the forecast date. In particle physics, going from ~3σ hints to a peer-reviewed ≥5σ claim that fast is very unlikely. My unconstrained estimate would be in the low single digits, but under the allowed floor I submit 5%.

Sherrill looks like a clear but not overwhelming favorite as of 2025-10-26. The best late polling mostly shows her ahead by 5-8 points and at or near 50%, with FDU, Rutgers-Eagleton, Quinnipiac, and Fox all pointing the same way, while Emerson alone showed a tie. Structural factors also help her: New Jersey still leans Democratic statewide, forecasters rate the race Leans Democratic, and at least one model had her around 82%. The main reasons not to go higher are that the race had reportedly tightened, Ciattarelli appeared to have stronger enthusiasm in an off-year turnout environment, and New Jersey gubernatorial races can be quirky and closer than the state’s federal lean suggests. Overall, that combination supports something close to an 80% chance rather than a near-lock.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the best available year-end evidence already pointed to FY2025 customs-duty collections of about $195b, not anywhere near the $500b lower bound. Both a report on CBO’s October estimate and a report summarizing Treasury’s final FY2025 Monthly Treasury Statement gave the same ~$195b figure, and Treasury’s total FY2025 receipts only rose by a few hundred billion overall. For this market to resolve YES, the later FY2025 Financial Report would need an extraordinary upward revision of more than $300b to the customs-duty line, which is far outside normal reporting revisions. Treasury also typically publishes the Financial Report before the Feb. 28 deadline, so nonpublication is not a likely route to ambiguity. I therefore put this at the minimum allowed probability.

Anthropic looks like a real but still clear underdog. The strongest pro-evidence is that Claude Sonnet 4.5 was released very close to the forecast date and a recent October source says it was already #1 on LMArena Text, which is directly relevant to the resolution source. Anthropic also benefits from the alphabetical tiebreak in any exact tie. But the negative side is substantial: earlier in 2025 Gemini was a strong leader, the frontier race is extremely volatile, rival labs remain highly active, and I never got a direct late-October score table to confirm the margin. Given those uncertainties, I put Anthropic at about one-in-five to be on top at the Dec. 31 check.

Kaiser looks like a clear long shot to lead Chile’s first presidential round as of the 2025-10-26 cutoff. The best late-October reporting puts him around 11-12% and in fourth place, behind Jara, Kast, and Matthei, while major coverage frames the race for first as mainly Jara vs. Kast. To win, Kaiser would need an unusually large late surge plus meaningful collapse or vote transfer from better-positioned right-wing rivals. But the right appears fragmented rather than consolidating behind him, and Kaiser had earlier refused coalition arrangements. Given those facts, I put him at the floor-like 5%: not impossible in a volatile multi-candidate race, but clearly unlikely.

Brooklyn looks like one of the NBA’s weakest teams as of the 2025-10-26 cutoff. Multiple preseason sources put the Nets at a 19.5 win total, +5000 just to make the playoffs, and +50000 to win the East, while broader league previews center Cleveland and New York as the main Eastern contenders. The Nets are widely described as rebuilding around a very young roster, with trade risk around Cam Thomas and little sign of near-term contender strength. My true estimate would be below 5%, but I’m using 5% because the task requires forecasts between 0.05 and 0.95.

Moonshot looks like a real frontier contender, but the pre-cutoff evidence points to it being outside the very top tier on Chatbot Arena heading into the final week. Kimi K2 reached the top 10 and beat DeepSeek, which keeps a YES outcome alive. But the stronger evidence is against: Moonshot was described as trailing the best commercial models, the late-September competitive set was still dominated by OpenAI/Google/Anthropic/xAI/DeepSeek, and Moonshot’s latest known update (K2-0905) still lacked reasoning/vision. With only five days from the cutoff to resolution, and no clear evidence of a late-October Moonshot release already poised to jump to #1, I put Moonshot’s chance in the high single digits rather than near zero, reflecting both Arena volatility and the lack of direct near-cutoff leaderboard data.

I put this at the floor, 5%. Outside view: Section 4 removal is unprecedented, and by the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff there were only about two months left in the resolution window. Inside view: the procedural bar is enormous — VP Vance and a Cabinet majority must initiate, and if Trump contests it Congress would need two-thirds in both chambers (search_0_summary). Reporting also described Vance and the Cabinet as strongly loyal to Trump, while Congress was Republican-dominated (search_0_summary). The most plausible path would be a sudden medical incapacitation event, but the latest mainstream reporting before the cutoff said Trump was in 'exceptional health' after a Walter Reed evaluation, with no indication of formal incapacity or hospitalization beyond routine testing (search_1_summary; search_3_result_0; search_3_result_2). There was public chatter and some Democratic calls to invoke the 25th Amendment, but no credible evidence by the cutoff of actual Cabinet movement toward Section 4. So YES remained extremely unlikely, though not impossible because an unforeseen catastrophic event could still occur before Dec. 31.

I estimate a very low chance of a Russian invasion of Finland before the end of 2025. The strongest evidence points to long-term military reconstitution and hybrid pressure, not imminent territorial seizure. NATO officials said the buildup near Finland was unlike the pre-2022 Ukraine invasion buildup (search_0_result_2). Finnish President Stubb said in October 2025 that Russia was not an 'imminent military threat' (search_1_result_9), and Finland's armed forces chief had earlier said Russia was unlikely to attack a NATO member in the coming years (search_1_result_3). Reuters reporting also frames the active 2025 confrontation as migration pressure, border fencing, airspace testing, and other hybrid incidents rather than attempts to seize Finnish land (search_4_result_0, search_4_result_4). The main argument for a nonzero risk is Russia's border buildup and hostile intent toward NATO (search_0_result_0, search_0_result_5), plus a small possibility of a deniable micro-incursion. But absent concrete attack indicators by Oct. 26, the probability for a qualifying offensive in the remaining weeks of 2025 is about 2%.

I estimate a 7% chance. By the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff, the latest reported 12-month CPI was only 3.0% in September after 2.9% in August, so inflation remained more than a full percentage point below the 4.1% resolution threshold with only October-December left to print. Late-2024 base effects offer some help, but not enough by themselves: November and December 2024 CPI were already 2.7%-2.9%, so a move to 4.1% would still require unusually hot monthly inflation in late 2025. The main upside risk is tariff pass-through and some acceleration in goods prices, and a few forecasters expected inflation to rise into year-end, but the available professional/Reuters consensus near the cutoff still centered around roughly 3.0%-3.1%, not anything close to 4%+. So YES needs a meaningful upside surprise in one of the last three releases, which looks possible but unlikely.

As of 2025-10-26, Kansas City looked like a strong but not dominant title contender. The best aggregate signal I found was that on Oct. 20 they were still the betting favorite for the Super Bowl at +500, roughly a mid-teens implied chance, despite an uneven start. Their case was helped by a rebound to 4-3, four wins in five games, Mahomes being healthy, and pass-catchers returning. Against that, they had opened 1-2, were only 4-3 by Week 8, trailed Denver in the division, and if the season had ended then they would have missed the playoffs. I therefore land near the market-implied mid-teens rather than higher.

Base rate for a 64-player single-elimination heads-up event is about 1.6% for an average entrant, and only a few percent even for a somewhat above-average player. Salsberg is confirmed in the field and has solid credentials, but by the cutoff there was no evidence of a deep run, while multiple pre-10/26 reports said the already-filmed event's winner had been leaked via prediction-market activity. One pre-cutoff report specifically highlighted Sam Soverel as the apparent leaked winner, which strongly implies Salsberg did not win. Given the instructions not to use post-cutoff confirmation and the model floor on probabilities, I land at 5%.

Avatar: Fire and Ash looks like a plausible Best Picture nominee but not a plausible Best Picture winner as of 2025-10-26. The strongest pro case is franchise pedigree: both prior Avatar films were nominated for Best Picture, and major awards outlets are at least listing it among contenders. But the strongest evidence is against a win: a major September predictions list had it only #9, Gold Derby’s framing is mostly ‘likely nominee / likely VFX force’ rather than ‘frontrunner,’ it opens very late on Dec. 19, and Oscars history is brutal to sequels—especially effects-heavy blockbusters—which are nominated more often than they actually win. Since this is an unconditional win forecast and a miss on nomination would already resolve No, I land at the minimum allowed probability.

Becoming Spotify’s global most-streamed artist for a year is an extremely high bar usually met by a tiny group of current-cycle megastars. The latest official anchor I found had Taylor Swift as Spotify’s 2024 global #1 and Eminem only #9 overall, so he was not entering 2025 from a near-leader position. I also found no clear evidence of a major 2025 Eminem release cycle comparable to the 2024 album rollout, and no reliable late-October 2025 source placing him near the top of year-to-date Spotify artist streams. With only about two months left in the year at the cutoff, a jump from outside the top tier to #1 looked very unlikely. I therefore put YES at the 5% floor.

Article 5 invocation is historically extraordinary—only once in NATO history—and as of the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff no member had invoked it despite a tense security environment. The strongest 2025 evidence is a cluster of Russian-linked drone and airspace incursions affecting Poland, Estonia, Denmark, and others, plus NATO’s acknowledgement that hybrid attacks could in theory trigger Article 5. But the key revealed-preference signal is that Poland handled the most serious September incident via Article 4, not Article 5, and Reuters/NATO reporting through Oct. 22 still described the incursions as being deterred with hybrid threats persisting, not as an armed attack requiring collective-defense activation. With about two months remaining, there is still a small tail risk of a deadlier or more clearly attributable attack on NATO territory, but the threshold for formal invocation appears very high. I therefore estimate a 5% chance of Article 5 being invoked by Dec. 31, 2025.

Billie Eilish is a plausible top-tier contender, but by late October 2025 the available evidence does not show her leading the race for Spotify’s year-end #1 artist. The title is historically concentrated among a tiny group of repeat streaming monsters, and late-2025 coverage points more toward Taylor Swift / Bad Bunny / The Weeknd than Billie. Billie’s 2025 story appears to be sustained momentum from her 2024 album and tour rather than a fresh 2025 blockbuster release cycle, which makes it hard to outrun those rivals over a full calendar year. Given that, I put her at the floor-ish end of plausible probabilities rather than literally zero.

An official OpenAI declaration of AGI by Dec. 31, 2025 still looks very unlikely. The outside view is already tiny: major AI labs almost never make clean public 'we achieved AGI' announcements, because the term is ambiguous and strategically costly. The inside view is only mildly supportive. Yes, Altman said in January that OpenAI knows how to build AGI, and o3 reportedly cleared an AGI-themed benchmark. But the more decision-relevant, recent evidence points the other way: Altman explicitly said in January they had not built AGI; by August he was downplaying AGI as an unhelpful binary term; OpenAI’s official definition remains demanding; and the company’s biggest 2025 launch moments—GPT-5 in August and DevDay/GPT-5 Pro in October—were framed as product and platform advances, not as AGI. With only about two months left after the Oct. 26 forecast date, and after those marquee opportunities passed without an AGI claim, I put the chance of an official announcement at 5%.

Monad had, by the Oct. 26 cutoff, moved well beyond vague airdrop speculation: there was an official post titled “The MON Airdrop,” a live eligibility/claim-check portal, and a short verification window ending Nov. 3. That makes an eventual MON token launch plus community distribution very likely, and it gives a decent chance of happening before Nov. 30. The main reason I’m below the mid-80s is that the most authoritative reporting (CoinDesk quoting the Monad Foundation) still said the actual airdrop, tokenomics, and mainnet launch would come at a later date, with no firm date disclosed as of mid-October. For this market, swappable tokens are required, so any slip to December or any locked/non-transferable distribution would resolve No. Net: more likely than not, but with meaningful deadline/slippage risk.

I put this at the floor, 5%. The outside view is that WHO declarations of a new coronavirus pandemic are extraordinarily rare. The specific Feb. 2025 HKU5-CoV-2 discovery was a lab finding in bats, not a human outbreak, and expert assessments consistently said there were no human infections, no evidence of human-to-human spread, and no immediate public-health threat. My searches through the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff did not surface any confirmed human outbreak of HKU5-CoV-2 or another novel coronavirus, nor any WHO pandemic-oriented action on a new coronavirus distinct from SARS-CoV-2. With only about two months remaining in 2025, a YES resolution would require a very fast-emerging outbreak plus WHO choosing to formally declare a pandemic before year-end. That is possible in principle but unlikely.

Best pre-cutoff evidence points to October 2025 being more likely above than below 1.10°C in NASA GISTEMP. September 2025 was still the third-warmest September globally and only about 0.07°C cooler than September 2024 in Copernicus, so there was no sign of a dramatic cooling into autumn. 2025 was also tracking among the top three warmest years on record through September, and NASA/GISS-related analysis argued temperatures were unlikely to fall much below ~1.5°C above the older preindustrial baseline even with La Niña/neutral conditions. That said, 2025 was cooler than the extreme 2023/2024 peaks and ENSO had eased to neutral, so a modest chance remains that NASA’s October value lands just under the 1.10°C cutoff. Overall I judge YES as unlikely but not impossible.

Kansas City still looked like a live contender as of 2025-10-26, but not the division favorite. Preseason markets had them only a narrow AFC West favorite (~52-53%), and by Oct. 19 they were just 4-3 and sitting third on tiebreaks after already losing once to both the Broncos and Chargers. That is meaningful negative evidence in a tight three-team race. The main reason I stay near, but below, a coin flip is that the Chiefs still had remaining head-to-head opportunities against both rivals and still possessed the Mahomes/Reid true-talent upside that made them the preseason favorite. Netting those together, I put them a bit under 50%.

By 2025-10-26, September had already delivered the first required leg: a 25 bp cut to 4.00%-4.25% (search_2_result_1). So the question effectively became: will October be a pause, and then December a cut? The problem is that essentially every strong late-October signal pointed the other way. Reuters reported futures implying near-100% odds of an October cut and 95% odds of another cut by December (search_0_result_3). A Reuters economist poll found 115 of 117 expecting a quarter-point cut in October (search_1_result_6). Even Fed communication leaned dovish for October: Waller favored a cut, and Powell said an October cut remained possible (search_3_result_7). Given that October pause looked like only a small-tail outcome, and the event needs that pause specifically, the true probability is likely below 5%; under the platform rule to avoid going below 5%, I submit 5%.

Moonshot looks like a real contender but still an outsider. The best pre-cutoff evidence I found has Kimi K2/K2-0905 in the Chatbot Arena top 10, yet only tied for 8th in September, not near #1. With only about two months from the Oct. 26 cutoff to Dec. 31, a lab starting from tied-8th needs either a major breakthrough release or a very compressed leaderboard. I found evidence of active iteration on K2-0905, but not of Moonshot already being in the top few or of a clearly imminent next-generation model likely to jump to #1. Meanwhile multiple stronger incumbents—OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, xAI, etc.—were still releasing rapidly. Given that the reference class is concentrated among a handful of frontier labs and Moonshot was outside the very top by the latest evidence, I put Moonshot's chance at the low end but not zero.

October 2025 landing specifically in NASA GISTEMP’s 1.10–1.14°C bracket looks unlikely. Recent October benchmarks are very high: October 2023 was the warmest October on record and October 2024 the second-warmest, only 0.02°C lower in NASA’s analysis (search_1_summary). September 2025 was still the third-warmest September on record and only 0.07°C cooler than September 2024 in Copernicus (search_2_summary), which suggests 2025 remains close to 2024 rather than experiencing the large cooling needed. ENSO was neutral by Sep/Oct 2025, not a stronger La Niña regime that might have pushed temperatures sharply down (search_2_summary). A baseline-conversion clue from RealClimate indicates GISTEMP’s standard 1951–1980 anomaly is roughly 0.19°C below a preindustrial-style anomaly (search_4_result_7); combined with NASA’s 1.55°C preindustrial-equivalent figure for October 2024, that implies October 2024 in the resolving NASA baseline was roughly mid-1.3s, well above the target band. So October 2025 would likely need around 0.2°C+ year-over-year cooling to hit 1.10–1.14°C, much more than the modest cooling seen in September. I therefore put this at the floor of my allowed range.

By the Oct. 26 cutoff, the evidence pointed overwhelmingly to an exactly 25 bp cut at the Oct. 28-29 FOMC meeting. Reuters reported 98% FedWatch odds on Oct. 3, then 97% odds on Oct. 22, and by Oct. 24 rate contracts implied near-100% certainty of a cut to 3.75%-4.00% (search_0_result_2, search_2_result_1, search_2_result_8). A Reuters poll found 115 of 117 economists expecting a 25 bp cut (search_0_result_1), and Governor Waller explicitly endorsed another 25 bp reduction in October (search_0_result_0). The latest CPI undershot expectations, which reinforced rather than undermined easing expectations (search_2_result_1). Residual uncertainty remains because market consensus is not certainty and elevated inflation/tariff concerns leave a small chance of no change; there is also a tiny chance of a larger move. But given the short horizon and near-unanimous market/economist/Fed signaling, YES is very likely.

By the Oct. 26 cutoff, Mamdani looked much more likely to finish first than second. He had already won the Democratic primary over Cuomo on the official BOE count (search_3_result_1), and multiple October general-election polls consistently put him in first place, usually with Cuomo in second: Quinnipiac 46-33-15 (search_0_result_3), Fox roughly 49-52 for Mamdani vs. 28 for Cuomo and low teens for Sliwa (search_0_result_7; search_4_result_7), and AARP/Gotham 43.2-28.9-19.4 (search_0_result_8). The main path to YES is a Cuomo upset where Mamdani still stays ahead of Sliwa/Adams, which is plausible but a tail scenario. Fragmentation among Cuomo, Sliwa, and Adams actually seems to protect Mamdani’s floor more than threaten it. So Mamdani finishing exactly second looks possible, but only around one chance in eleven.

Le Havre looked like a relegation-battle team, not a title contender, both before and during the season. Preseason models had them around 16th and fighting survival, and by the 2025-10-26 cutoff they were reportedly 16th with 6 points from 8 matches, including a 6-2 loss to Marseille; clubs in that position almost never recover to win Ligue 1, especially with PSG/Marseille still setting a solid pace. Given the minimum allowed forecast is 5%, I’m submitting the floor.

Walter Reeves appears to have been an early prospective challenger, but by late September and October 2025 multiple authoritative pre-election sources list the qualified Atlanta mayoral candidates as Andre Dickens, Helmut Domagalski, Kalema Jackson, and Eddie Andrew Meredith — not Reeves. That makes Reeves very likely not on the ballot. Even if there were some extraordinary write-in path, Dickens is the incumbent frontrunner with major fundraising and incumbency advantages. So Reeves’s win chance is at the floor of the allowed range.

xAI is a live but clearly secondary contender. The strongest positive is that Grok has already shown it can top Chatbot Arena at least temporarily, and xAI appears to be shipping quickly enough that another year-end jump is plausible. But the best mid/late-2025 competitive evidence still has xAI trailing Google/OpenAI on the relevant leaderboard, while xAI also suffers from the alphabetical tiebreak and some evidence that Grok looks less strong under style-controlled Arena analysis. With only about two months from the cutoff to resolution, I think xAI has a real but modest chance to overtake and hold #1 by the check time.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the first leg was already satisfied: the Fed cut 25 bps in September. But the required second leg—an October pause—looked highly unlikely. Multiple late-October Reuters reports said economists and markets overwhelmingly expected another 25 bp cut on Oct. 29, with one Reuters poll showing 115 of 117 economists forecasting a cut and futures implying near-100% odds of easing. Evidence also leaned toward another December cut rather than a pause. So YES required the Fed to defy a near-consensus October-cut expectation and then also pause in December. That seems like a very low-probability tail outcome, near the floor.

Direct U.S.-Venezuela state-on-state kinetic clashes are historically rare, and the strongest reporting before Oct. 26 still describes the actual strikes as against alleged drug/fishing/semi-submersible vessels rather than Venezuelan military forces. That means the market likely had not already resolved YES. However, the situation was genuinely dangerous: repeated U.S. strikes near Venezuela, Venezuelan wartime alert, close military encounters, and a late-Oct. carrier deployment created a meaningful tail risk that the final five days could cross the threshold. Net: elevated but still clearly below 50%, about 21%.

I estimate 9%. By late October 2025, the U.S. had already escalated to real military action around Venezuela: repeated maritime strikes, a sizable Caribbean buildup, and authorized CIA covert operations all make further escalation plausible. But this market resolves only if the U.S. begins an offensive intended to establish control over Venezuelan territory, and the strongest reporting I found consistently points instead to maritime interdiction, possible land strikes, or covert/special operations—not a seizure-and-hold mission. With only about two months remaining, and experts saying the current force is enough to strike but not to seize terrain, YES remains a meaningful tail risk but still clearly less likely than NO.

I estimate an 8% chance. The June ceasefire was clearly fragile and conditional, and Israeli leaders kept signaling willingness to strike Iran again if threatened. But by Oct. 26 the stronger evidence points to restraint over the relevant 5-day horizon: the direct war had been paused since late June, Reuters-sourced material I found pointed to rhetoric and contingency planning rather than renewed direct attacks, Iranian capabilities had been degraded, and U.S. pressure favored diplomacy. With no clear late-October public catalyst for immediate state-to-state aerial strikes, YES is possible but still unlikely.

I estimate a 5% chance. The outside view for a new U.S. offensive intended to seize and hold territory from a large sovereign adversary over a ~2 month remaining horizon is extremely low. The inside view also points down: when the June 2025 crisis peaked, the U.S. did strike Iran directly, but senior officials repeatedly framed it as a limited strike, not the start of a ground war, and explicitly said there would not be U.S. boots on the ground. Reuters reporting also shows the conflict moved quickly toward a ceasefire and that Iran’s retaliation on a U.S. base was calibrated and caused no U.S. casualties, which is more consistent with de-escalation than a path to occupation. I found no credible evidence of late-2025 U.S. ground-force preparations or plans to seize Iranian territory; reported force movements were air-power reinforcements, which fit deterrence/strike options better than invasion. The main residual risk is a new extreme trigger before year-end, or a very limited seizure of a small site or island that would still satisfy the resolution criteria.

The Weeknd looked like a real but secondary contender by the 2025-10-26 cutoff. Positives: he finished #2 on Spotify Wrapped 2024 and had a strong January 2025 album cycle with big opening Spotify numbers, so he clearly belonged in the small top-tier contender set. Negatives: Taylor Swift entered 2025 as the back-to-back global top artist, and Bad Bunny appears to have had an even stronger 2025-specific catalyst with a huge January album and favorable late-2025 betting/industry commentary. Pre-cutoff indirect indicators consistently center Bad Bunny and Taylor Swift as the main 2025 race, with The Weeknd not treated as the favorite. So I give him a low but non-negligible chance, around 9%.

Polymarket had already crossed the hardest hurdles by the cutoff: it acquired a licensed U.S. DCM/clearing venue, received a CFTC green light/no-action path, filed self-certifications for initial sports and election contracts, and was repeatedly described by major outlets as preparing to launch within days. Those facts make a 2025 U.S. go-live more likely than not. The main reason I am below the market price is that, by late October, I still do not see clear independent confirmation that public real-money trading had actually begun, and several reports indicated a government-shutdown-related delay plus continued waitlist/future-tense language. With about two months left after the cutoff, I still think launch by Dec. 31 is likely, but not near-certain.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the main direct US-Iran clash in the resolution window had already happened: the US struck Iranian nuclear sites, Iran retaliated against a US base in Qatar, and a ceasefire followed. Yet credible reporting through late October still described US action as air/naval/regional force posture, not regular ground troops entering Iran. US officials also publicly disclaimed interest in boots on the ground. Since more than four months of the window had elapsed without qualifying entry, the remaining path to YES was mostly a low-probability contingency such as a rescue or retaliatory raid in the last two months. That keeps the probability at the minimum allowed 5%, close to the market's 5% signal.

As of 2025-10-26, Minecraft had already posted a finalized $163M 3-day domestic opening, and the biggest released challengers through summer were still well short of that mark. The remaining credible threats were mostly late-2025 titles—Wicked: For Good, Zootopia 2, and Avatar: Fire and Ash. Late-October chatter suggested Wicked around $155M and Zootopia somewhere between roughly $115-130M on one source and $155-190M on another, with likely holiday-frame/3-day ambiguity for Zootopia. Avatar was clearly a major franchise threat, but I did not find strong pre-cutoff evidence that it was more likely than not to clear $163M on a 3-day basis. So Minecraft looked more likely than not to keep the crown, but with meaningful downside from a few Q4 tentpoles.

Google looks more likely than not to resolve YES because the best pre-cutoff evidence suggests Gemini 2.5 Pro was still leading Chatbot Arena text into late summer/early fall 2025, and Google also had unusual depth near the top with multiple strong Gemini variants. That said, I could not verify an authoritative Oct. 26 leaderboard snapshot, and late-2025 rival risk is real: GPT-5 was already strong on benchmarks and xAI was signaling Grok 5 before year-end. So Google is the single most likely winner, but not overwhelmingly so.

By 2025-10-26, WHO had not already made a disease-specific pandemic declaration that surfaced in targeted WHO-site searches, and the main plausible candidates looked short of pandemic criteria. Mpox was still being handled under PHEIC/strategic-response language, while WHO and other authoritative summaries said avian influenza still did not transmit easily person-to-person and human cases remained mostly animal-exposure driven. With only about two months left in 2025, the remaining hazard of WHO both seeing a sudden major escalation and formally declaring a disease a pandemic appears very low, though not zero because H5N1 pandemic potential was still being discussed.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Starmer was clearly weakened but still PM, with no formal challenge underway and only about two months left in the window. The strongest pro-YES evidence is severe polling weakness, ministerial turbulence, and open internal dissent. But the strongest anti-YES evidence is more important for this short horizon: Labour’s large Commons majority, no active formal mechanism in motion, high nomination thresholds for a challenge, and the main rumored rival (Burnham) not being an MP. That makes a forced or voluntary exit before year-end possible but still distinctly unlikely. I therefore land near 9%, close to the contemporaneous market level.

John Smith was a confirmed entrant in a 64-player single-elimination field, and he is not a random amateur—coverage notes he was twice runner-up in the WSOP heads-up championship. On pure ex ante skill/base-rate grounds, that might have put him a bit above the ~1.6% neutral-entry baseline. But by the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the most important evidence was that this event had already been recorded and multiple pre-cutoff reports said prediction-market trading appeared to leak the result, with Sam Soverel trading around 90-99% as the apparent likely winner. Combined with the prompt’s note that John Smith’s own market price was 0.00 on 2025-10-16, the balance of evidence strongly suggests Smith did not win. Because that evidence is still indirect rather than an official published result, I stay at the minimum allowed forecast rather than going even lower.

I put this near the floor at 5%. Z.ai is clearly a real lab with momentum — it is Zhipu/Z.ai, and it launched GLM-4.5 in late July 2025 as a serious frontier/open model effort (search_1_result_1; search_2_result_6). But the best concrete comparative signal I found does not show it as the top overall model: even favorable coverage says GLM-4.5 was 3rd on a combined benchmark set, behind leading OpenAI and Anthropic models (search_2_result_4). Separately, pre-cutoff reporting around LM Arena indicates OpenAI's GPT-5 was ranking first across LM Arena categories in August, implying the incumbent leader was a major frontier rival rather than Z.ai (search_5_result_2). With only five days from cutoff to resolution, absent any evidence that a Z.ai/GLM model was already sitting at or near #1 on the relevant Arena leaderboard, the path to YES would require either an unobserved near-top standing or a very fast late jump. That is possible but unlikely. The alphabetical tiebreak also works against Z.ai in ties versus most major competitors (search_2_summary).

A >$250B Q4/Q4 drop in FGEXPND would require roughly a 3.5% nominal decline from a ~$7T SAAR spending base. By October 2025, the strongest available aggregate evidence pointed the other way: CBO estimated FY2025 federal outlays were up about $301B (4%) year over year, or up about $228B (3%) after timing adjustments, with major mandatory programs and interest still rising. Although FGEXPND is not identical to Treasury/CBO outlays and a quarter-specific dip remains possible, the broad fiscal picture makes a >$250B fall in Q4 2025 current expenditures very unlikely.

I estimate a 14% chance. This is now a real contender: Taylor Swift’s album is already released, debuted at No. 1 with a record 4.002 million units, and stayed No. 1 in week two with 338,000 units and a huge lead over No. 2. That puts it in the small class of albums capable of a very long reign. But 17 straight weeks is an extreme bar: Swift’s closest comparator, The Tortured Poets Department, reached 17 weeks at No. 1 only nonconsecutively after an initial 12-week streak, and Billboard history suggests even 17 total weeks atop is very rare. The late-2025 release calendar looks fairly light, which helps, but there are still roughly 15 more weeks of exposure to decay, holiday effects, and surprise major releases. So the odds are materially above the pre-release base rate, but still firmly in the low-teens.

DeepSeek is clearly in the frontier pack, so this is not a near-zero event. But the outside view is unfavorable: several elite labs are competing for a single #1 Arena slot, and only about two months remained from the cutoff to resolution. The strongest concrete leaderboard evidence I found points to Google/Gemini leading the main Arena earlier in 2025 rather than DeepSeek, while DeepSeek’s strongest late-September confirmed release was described by Reuters as an intermediate experimental step toward later-generation models, not the flagship R2/V4 jump. That leaves a real but limited path: DeepSeek could still win if it launches a strong next-gen model before year-end or if its existing models are already closer to #1 than my sources revealed. The alphabetical tiebreak is mildly favorable for DeepSeek against Google/OpenAI/xAI, but not enough to offset the lack of evidence that it was the incumbent leader near the cutoff. Net: low single-digit YES odds, higher than the 1% market quote but still clearly unlikely.

I’d put this slightly above even odds. As of the cutoff, BTC had already shown it could get very close to the barrier: Reuters reported a drop to $104,782 on Oct. 10, only ~4.8% above the trigger, and the selloff was liquidation-driven, which matters because a single 1-minute Binance wick is enough for YES. By Oct. 25, though, BTC had stabilized around $111k, so a further ~10% decline was still needed. That kind of move is common enough for Bitcoin over a 2-month horizon, but strong ETF/institutional support and lower leverage than earlier in the year argue against making YES too high. Net: modest lean to YES, not a slam dunk.

I estimate a 5% chance Musk is TIME's 2025 Person of the Year. He was undeniably influential in 2025—TIME profiled him extensively and Reuters described him as deeply involved in Trump-era politics and later breaking with Trump. But the evidence up to 2025-10-26 points more strongly to other candidates or themes: Trump, AI, and Pope Leo XIV repeatedly appear ahead of Musk in contender coverage, and TIME's own editorial focus seems to frame 2025 around broader AI and rival political/religious narratives rather than Musk alone. The prompt's near-cutoff market estimate of 4% is also an important anchor. Since 5% is the minimum allowed and roughly matches the balance of evidence, I land there.

I estimate a 10% chance. By the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff, The Life of a Showgirl had already debuted at #1 and logged three straight weeks on top, with a historically massive 4.002M-unit opening for Taylor Swift. That clearly gives it a real path. But 16 consecutive weeks is an extreme outcome on the Billboard 200: even very dominant recent albums usually top out below that, and Swift’s own recent blockbuster The Tortured Poets Department reached 12 straight weeks rather than 16. Showgirl’s week-to-week totals also collapsed quickly after release, from 4.002M to 338K to 194K, so it would need to stabilize for 13 more weeks while avoiding any meaningful challenger through late 2025 and into January. The apparently soft release calendar helps, but the historical rarity and required duration still make YES unlikely overall.

By Oct. 26, the leading actual 2025 opener appears to be A Minecraft Movie at about $163M domestic, which is a very high hurdle. Authoritative long-range tracking for Zootopia 2 is only about $95M-$110M for the 3-day, with more bullish unofficial tracking around $115M-$130M. Thanksgiving can drive huge totals, but its Fri-Sun 3-day is not obviously advantaged enough to beat $163M; even Moana 2’s record Thanksgiving 3-day was about $139.7M. With Wicked also tracking strongly and Avatar still a residual risk, Zootopia 2 needs a substantial upside surprise to finish #1 for the year. I put that at about 10%.

Most political shootings with an identified suspect end up classified as lone-actor attacks, and the case-specific evidence points that way here. Multiple contemporaneous reports said officials believed Robinson acted alone, including NYT/NBC/live press coverage (search_2_result_1, search_2_result_4, search_2_result_6). The main counterevidence is that on Sept. 21 the FBI said it was still investigating the possibility of accomplices (search_1_result_9). But by late-September reporting, no additional suspects or co-conspirators had been named, and later coverage emphasized false conspiracy claims rather than real accomplice evidence (search_4_result_0). Given the short horizon from Oct. 26 to Oct. 31 and the lack of concrete public evidence of collaborators, I think law enforcement was most likely to resolve this as a lone-actor case.

OpenAI looks like a real contender but not the clear favorite. Late-2025 sources describe Chatbot Arena’s top tier as crowded with Google, xAI, Anthropic, DeepSeek, and OpenAI all in the mix, while the Arena leader can change quickly after new releases. That volatility gives OpenAI some upside, but the evidence we found does not show OpenAI as the incumbent #1 near the cutoff; tie-breaks work against it versus Google and Anthropic, Elo accumulation has some lag, and at least one major rival (xAI) was publicly signaling another release before year-end. Net: modest but clearly sub-20% chance.

By the Oct. 26 cutoff, the best available evidence suggests BTC had not come close to $90k in October and was trading around $111k-$114k heading into the final week. Reuters reported the major October washout only reached about $104,783 on Oct. 10-11 (search_0_result_1), and CoinDesk reported BTC was still above $111k on Oct. 25 with nearby technical support around $108k, leaving roughly a 19% gap to $90k (search_4_summary). A move that large in under a week is possible in crypto, especially since BTC already dropped >14% in a day earlier in October (search_0_result_1), and derivatives markets showed heavy downside hedging after that crash (search_5_summary). But absent evidence that a fresh acute shock was already underway by Oct. 26, a further slide from ~$111k-$114k to $90k before month-end looked unlikely. I therefore put the YES probability at 5.5%.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Francesca Muñoz appears effectively unable to win. Multiple Chilean news sources reported on June 30/July 1 that she withdrew her presidential candidacy and endorsed José Antonio Kast, and a September Servel-based candidate list did not include her among the eight official candidates. Separate October coverage and polling-focused articles consistently framed the race around Jara, Kast, Matthei, and Kaiser, with no sign Muñoz was a serious contender. Inside-view probability is essentially near zero; I use 5% only because the allowed forecast range has a floor.

By Oct. 26, 2025 there is clearly a real US-Venezuela military crisis: US forces have been operating for weeks in the Caribbean, Trump has openly discussed strikes on land in Venezuela, Maduro has mobilized forces, and there have already been multiple US strikes on suspected drug vessels near Venezuela. That said, the best high-authority reporting available here still suggests that the known kinetic actions have mostly targeted alleged drug-trafficking vessels rather than clearly identified Venezuelan military forces, and the known US-Venezuelan military encounter (the F-16 flyby of a US destroyer) did not involve exchanged fire. So the market hinges mainly on whether, in the remaining few days before Oct. 31, rhetoric/posturing turns into either (a) a US strike on Venezuelan land territory, which would automatically resolve YES, or (b) an accidental or deliberate direct clash at sea/air. Given Trump's public signaling, that risk is substantial but still less than even odds, especially because after eight weeks of operations the parties had not yet crossed the resolution threshold and land strikes still appeared contingent rather than underway.

Valencia look like an extreme long shot. The outside view is already brutal: since 2004, no club outside Real Madrid, Barcelona, or Atletico has won La Liga, and Valencia themselves have not been near title level for years. The inside view through the 2025-10-26 cutoff is no better: Opta’s preseason model projected Valencia 10th on 49 points and outside even the fringe title group, while mid-October betting/preview coverage described a slow start, put them in the bottom half, and framed the title race around Real Madrid and Barcelona instead. With no evidence that Valencia were near the top of the table or priced as a serious contender, the permitted floor of 5% is already generous.

By the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff, authoritative Billboard reporting already indicated that Taylor Swift’s The Life of a Showgirl was released on Oct. 3, 2025 and debuted at No. 1 on the Billboard 200 dated Oct. 18, 2025, with 4.002 million equivalent album units; Billboard also reported it had reached a third consecutive week at No. 1 by Oct. 26 (search_0_result_2, search_0_result_0, search_0_summary). Since the market resolves YES only if the album is #1 for 0 straight weeks—i.e., it never debuts at No. 1 or is unreleased by year-end—this evidence makes YES extremely unlikely. I assign the minimum allowed 5% only to cover residual possibility of source/snippet ambiguity, not because the underlying chart outcome appears genuinely uncertain.

Drake remained a plausible but remote contender: he is historically one of the few artists capable of winning and had some 2025 momentum, including a new release and short-term Spotify chart strength. But the stronger evidence points against him. Spotify Wrapped 2024 had him only 4th globally, recent annual winners were Taylor Swift and Bad Bunny rather than Drake, and the best 2025 year-to-date proxy I found also placed him 4th behind Bad Bunny, Taylor Swift, and The Weeknd. With the cutoff in late October, only a few weeks remained for a dramatic reversal, so YES looked very unlikely, though not impossible given source ambiguity and Drake’s scale.

As of 2025-10-26, a YES would require an extremely fast and historically unusual shift from debate to an independently controlled operational weapon, plus credible confirmation by year-end. The strongest candidate pool looks weak: Saudi Arabia likely is out of scope because it was still only under consideration for MNNA status, while Japan’s visible movement is toward nuclear sharing rather than independent control, and South Korea still appears constrained to hedging/latency discussions under U.S. nonproliferation pressure rather than weapon possession. I see no credible pre-cutoff reporting that any in-scope ally already possesses or has assembled a device. My unconstrained estimate would be a bit below 5%, but I submit 5% because that is the allowed floor.

By Oct. 26, there is strong evidence RFK Jr. wanted to make this kind of announcement: he promised in April to identify the cause of autism by September, reiterated in August that he was 'on track,' and September reporting suggested an upcoming report might point to Tylenol or other causes (search_0_result_0, search_0_summary, search_1_summary, search_2_summary). But the stronger, more outcome-relevant evidence points the other way: the timeline had already slipped from September to March, NIH leadership cautioned that science could not meet the political deadline, Reuters described the initiative as still in a proposal/data-gathering phase in early September, and repeated late-period coverage still discussed only possible causes/upcoming reports rather than any actual definitive public announcement (search_0_result_4, search_1_result_2, search_3_summary, search_2_summary). With no clear announcement found by Oct. 26 and only a few days left until Nov. 1, the remaining chance looks low but not zero because RFK Jr. could still make a politically motivated unsupported claim at the last minute.

Peru appears to have been mathematically eliminated from CONMEBOL qualifying by early September 2025 after a 3-0 loss to Uruguay, with only one match left and only Venezuela/Bolivia still alive for the playoff spot. Under the market rules, once it becomes impossible for Peru to win the World Cup, the outcome is effectively NO. Since forecasts are bounded away from zero, I submit the minimum allowed 5%.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the strongest evidence said 2025 was still exceptionally warm but generally running behind 2024, with only three months left to make up the gap. Multiple sources described 2025 as on pace for the second- or third-warmest year, not the warmest, and September 2025 itself was not a record month. Berkeley Earth explicitly estimated only a 6% chance that 2025 would finish as the warmest year. Because the market resolves on NASA GISTEMP specifically, I allow a small tail probability for dataset differences or an unusually hot Oct-Dec, but the most likely outcome is that 2024 remains the record-holder.

xAI is a real contender, but the best concrete evidence I found for the relevant text Chatbot Arena leaderboard has Google’s Gemini 2.5 Pro ahead of xAI’s Grok 4 by a substantial 36-point margin as of Aug. 19, 2025. With only 5 days from cutoff to resolution, a comeback usually requires a fresh text-leaderboard-leading release. xAI did release Grok 4 Fast in September, but the evidence I found only shows it topping Search Arena, not the resolving text leaderboard. I found no authoritative late-October text-leaderboard snapshot showing xAI in front. Given xAI’s demonstrated ability to hit #1 earlier in 2025, YES is still possible, but absent stronger near-cutoff evidence it looks unlikely.

By the Oct. 26 cutoff, the strongest and most recent evidence points to Tesla still not having a qualifying unsupervised public launch. June/July reporting showed Austin robotaxis used in-vehicle safety monitors or 'babysitters,' so the milestone had not been met midyear. More importantly, Tesla's own Oct. 22 earnings-call reporting said it was operating supervised fleets in Austin and multiple cities and expected no safety drivers only by the end of 2025, which is after the Oct. 31 deadline. Tesla also still marketed consumer FSD as 'Full Self-Driving (Supervised)' in September, making a surprise qualifying software rollout to customer cars less likely. There is a small tail risk of a last-minute Austin transition in the final week, but given the near-cutoff official language, NO is overwhelmingly more likely.

By the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff, the Bucharest by-election had been set for Dec. 7, but Victor Ponta did not appear in the main late-stage candidate discussion or polling. Repeated local and national reporting instead centered figures like Daniel Băluță, Ciprian Ciucu, and sometimes Cătălin Drulă, while Ponta was absent. For a specifically named politician to win, I would usually want at least a declared candidacy, serious media treatment as a contender, or polling inclusion by late October. I found none of those, so I keep him at a low residual chance rather than zero.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the first leg was already satisfied: the Fed cut 25 bp in September. An October cut looked extremely likely, with futures around 86% immediately after September, rising to roughly 95% by early/mid-October, and Reuters describing another October cut as broadly expected. The decisive issue was December. On that front, the evidence before the cutoff leaned strongly against a pause: Reuters on Oct. 3 said CME FedWatch implied a 90% likelihood of another December cut, September futures priced about 70 bp of remaining 2025 easing, and a Reuters poll published Oct. 22 said economists expected cuts both in October and December. There was some contrary support for a pause — notably BofA pulling its final 2025 cut forward into October, which implies December pause in that house view — but that looked like a minority view versus broader market/economist expectations. So the most likely path as of the cutoff was Cut–Cut–Cut, not Cut–Cut–Pause. A low-teens probability best reflects that October was likely but December pause was a clear underdog.

SOL reaching $350 by the end of October looks like a low-probability tail event. The strongest evidence is that as of Oct. 25 SOL was only about $191.95 and struggling around the $192-$195 resistance zone, so a touch of $350 would require roughly an 82% jump in under a week (search_3_result_4). That threshold is also materially above Solana’s prior ATH of about $295.31 (search_3_result_5), so this is not just asking for a normal bullish continuation but for a new blowoff move. There were real bullish catalysts — bitcoin hit fresh record highs and crypto ETF inflows were strong (search_2_result_0, search_2_result_2), while Solana ETF approval looked likely in October (search_1_result_1, search_1_result_9). But reporting also said expected Solana ETF inflows were modest (search_1_result_8), and contemporaneous market commentary still had SOL trading near $190-$216 with technical targets/resistance far below $350 (search_0_result_1, search_0_result_2, search_0_result_7). A one-minute Binance wick makes YES slightly more plausible than a sustained trade at $350, but it still appears very unlikely by the cutoff date.

Bad Bunny looks more likely than not—and substantially so—to finish as Spotify’s top artist for 2025, but I’m not willing to go all the way to the 0.91 market price given the limited direct artist-level YTD evidence I found. The strongest pro-YES evidence is that by October his 2025 album 'DeBÍ TiRAR MáS FOToS' had become the first album of 2025 to pass 7 billion Spotify streams, which strongly suggests massive sustained platform dominance rather than a short-lived spike. Independent odds coverage in late September also had him as the favorite. The main risk is Taylor Swift: she was the incumbent winner in 2023 and 2024, has a huge catalog, and had a new October release that could drive a late surge. Still, with only a relatively short period left before Wrapped-era counting likely closes, Bad Bunny’s apparent yearlong streaming momentum makes him the likeliest winner. I therefore land at 85%.

Biedermann still held the men's long-course 200m freestyle record on the cutoff date, and this 1:42.00 mark has proven exceptionally durable since 2009. The strongest 2025 context I found suggests the event was still well off record pace: SwimSwam framed the 2025 Worlds as potentially producing only a 1:43 winner, and recent fast swims like Hwang Sunwoo's 1:43.92 were still far from 1:42.00. So the short-horizon chance that Wikipedia will list a new holder by early November is very low. I trim a bit for later dates because the event is prestigious and any additional long-course meet creates some chance of a breakthrough or pending-ratification mark appearing on Wikipedia.

I start from the 0.68 empirical base rate for FIDE ranking persistence, then discount it because Hans is sitting right on the apparent benchmark (#15) and the nearby ratings are tightly bunched, so small rating changes can move him several places. The prompt and pre-cutoff Wikipedia page both point to a benchmark very likely being about #15, which keeps the near-term probability a bit above even. The first two resolution dates likely depend on the same 1 Nov Wikipedia/FIDE list, so they should be very close; I give 2025-11-02 a tiny extra bump because there is some chance the page still shows the October list on that date. By 2026-01-24, extra months of play and peer movement make the outcome meaningfully less predictable, so I move it closer to 0.5.
