Wikipedia’s source page still showed the HPIV/croup vaccine field blank as of the 2025-10-01 revision, and the prompt says the most recent known status as of 2025-10-16 was No. The main question is whether that flips to Yes within 1 week, 1 month, or ~3 months. Outside-view, first vaccines for longstanding respiratory viruses almost never appear suddenly; they are usually preceded by conspicuous late-stage trial success or approvals. The evidence I found points the other way: a 2024 PNAS paper says HPIV3 lacks an approved vaccine, CDC’s 2024 HPIV clinical overview does not indicate any available vaccine, and the only concrete trial evidence surfaced was an HPIV2 phase I candidate that was overattenuated rather than near approval. There is active research, so I do not put the chance at zero, and the longer horizon gets a slightly higher probability. But a Wikipedia switch from No to Yes by these dates still looks unlikely.

Human bocavirus infection was still effectively at 'No vaccine' status on the relevant Wikipedia list page at the cutoff, and the latest known value before cutoff was also No. The outside view for an obscure pathogen with no listed vaccine flipping to Yes within 1 week to 3 months is extremely low. The strongest direct evidence I found is negative: a 2023 medical overview says there is no vaccine available and no vaccine in development for human use (search_0_result_3), and a 2025 comprehensive review does not indicate any approved vaccine or obvious late-stage candidate (search_0_result_5). The only positive evidence is much older preclinical work showing mouse immunogenicity of VP2 VLPs, i.e. candidate concepts rather than a developed vaccine (search_0_result_0). So I assign very low Yes probabilities, rising only slightly with time because longer horizons allow a bit more chance of an overlooked or newly recognized vaccine and because forecasts should be less extreme farther out.

The resolution source itself showed Human ewingii ehrlichiosis with no vaccine listed as of the cutoff-era Wikipedia revision I fetched. Additional context also points strongly to NO: CDC said in May 2025 that there is no vaccine to prevent ehrlichiosis, and the search results only surfaced preclinical/candidate-vaccine work for other Ehrlichia species (especially E. chaffeensis and E. canis), not a developed vaccine for E. ewingii. Over a 1-week to 3-month horizon, a change from no listed vaccine to a developed vaccine for this obscure tick-borne bacterial disease is extremely unlikely. I give only a slight increase with longer horizons to reflect small chances of a rapid scientific or Wikipedia-status update.

King is still the listed holder on the cutoff-date Wikipedia page, and late-July 2025 reporting still described her as the 100m breaststroke world-record holder after the 2025 World Championships—the most obvious 2025 long-course record opportunity had already passed without the record falling. The event is competitive and the record is old, but the evidence I found does not show anyone sitting right on top of 1:04.13, and the post-October calendar is much less favorable for long-course world records than the summer championship season. So I put very high odds on survival through early November, with only a modest decline by late January because more time always allows more chances for an upset or a pending record to appear on Wikipedia.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Wikipedia still lists Ariarne Titmus as the women's long-course 200m freestyle world-record holder at 1:52.23. The biggest recent opportunity for the record to fall—the 2025 World Championships in late July—passed without it happening: Mollie O'Callaghan won the event, but the record survived. That is strong evidence because O'Callaghan appears to be the most credible immediate threat. The main downside risk is that O'Callaghan was described as the #2 all-time performer and fastest woman in the world that season, so there is a real, though limited, chance she or another elite swimmer lowers the mark at a later meet. Given the very short first two horizons and the fact that the main 2025 long-course championship window had already passed by the cutoff, I keep the probabilities high, then shade downward by January 2026 to reflect more calendar time for an upset.

Base rate is extremely low for +1% FIDE Elo questions, and here +1% from an October rating near 2473 means needing roughly 2498. Vaishali is a stronger-than-average candidate relative to the base rate because she is young, elite, active, and just won the 2025 Women’s Grand Swiss. But that same Grand Swiss ended on Sept. 15, and the Wikipedia/FIDE page still showed her at 2473 on the Oct. 1 list, which suggests that big result was already not enough to put her near the threshold. I found no concrete pre-cutoff evidence that she was already near 2498 or had a clear slate of additional rated classical games before the November list. So the near-term dates remain very unlikely. Nov. 25 is only slightly higher than Nov. 2 because it is mostly the same November rating question but with less Wikipedia-update risk. By Jan. 24 she has a couple more monthly lists/opportunities to gain rating, so I raise the probability modestly, but a 25-point gain at this level is still uncommon.

Reference class: month-to-month/top-list ranking persistence for elite chess players on the FIDE classical list. The generic base rate is 0.68, but Anand’s specific setup is weaker than average because he is sitting exactly around the threshold: #13 on the October 2025 list with only a 1-point edge over #14 and several players close behind. That makes a drop by one spot quite plausible if nearby rivals have a modestly positive event. Offsetting that, elite rankings are fairly sticky, FIDE updates only monthly, and Anand is already established at the target rank. For 2025-11-02 I give a modest extra bump because it is so close to the cutoff and there is some chance Wikipedia is still effectively reflecting the October list. By 2025-11-25, I assume the November list is more likely to be reflected, so the probability is a bit lower. By 2026-01-24 there is more time for active players just behind him to pass 2743 or for inactivity/relative drift to matter, so the estimate moves closer to 0.5.

Reference class: elite-player FIDE/Wikipedia monthly-rating questions asking for a +1% Elo increase from a fixed baseline. The supplied empirical base rate is 0.007, but the key question-specific issue is the baseline. The Wikipedia page fetched at the cutoff still showed the 1 October 2025 list with Gukesh at 2752, and contemporaneous reporting also says his official October 2025 rating was 2752 after a poor Grand Swiss. The widely quoted 2794 appears to refer to October 2024, not his current October 2025 rating. So the practical target is likely about 2780, not 2822.
